context.description: This nonfiction page discusses how dream symbols arise from waking consciousness and are shaped into symbolic dream imagery. It then speculates whether a similar process may occur in waking life through the superego, before shifting into a critical transition under the heading "Es ist nicht alles Gold was glänzt."
normalization_notes: Mapped raw book/philosophical writing to book_body_content/theoretical_discussion.
Translated section heading into English for retrieval tags only; source heading remains visible in content.

fields.section_heading: Es ist not all gold that glitters

rows.paragraph_text: Dream symbols are presented as fragments of waking consciousness that enter dreams, lose their original structure, and become symbolic imagery. The text argues that the content of symbolic meaning matters less than the process by which it is formed.
The discussion then extends the analogy to waking life, asking whether parts of the superego may likewise shape consciousness and reality in fragmentary, symbolized form.
A new section begins with a critical warning about the limits and contradictions of such analogies.

content.free_text: book_page book_body_content theoretical_discussion Book page on dream symbolism, waking consciousness, and the superego. The text explains how symbolic form arises and introduces a critical section on the limits of analogy.
